html:
RECOUP Centre of Excellence for Integrative, Root-Cause Rheumatological Rehabilitation

Introduction

  • Arthritis is a joint inflammation that can cause stiffness or pain.
  • There are over 100 different types of arthritis.
  • Arthritis can occur in all age groups, including children.
  • Osteoarthritis (OA) is a chronic degenerative joint disease that causes articular cartilage to break down.
  • Rheumatoid Arthritis (RA) is an autoimmune disease that targets joints, causing pain, inflammation and joint destruction.
  • RA can affect all joints, including hands, wrists, knees, shoulders and spine, often with a symmetrical presentation.
  • Arthritis is widespread in India, affecting more than 180 million people. The most common type of arthritis in India is OA, which affects 22–39% of the population. RA affects 0.5–1% of the population.
  • Knee OA, characterized by knee pain and functional limitation, is widely understood to imply that symptoms are due to structural damage ("wear and tear" or "bone on bone").
  • This view leads to the belief that non-surgical approaches are futile and a total knee replacement must fix the structural damage.
  • In contrast, contemporary evidence supports knee OA as a 'whole person condition' in which knee health is influenced by several biological (thigh muscle weakness, inflammation), psychological (low self-efficacy, unhelpful beliefs, stress, depressed mood), social (work and sport demands), and lifestyle (poor sleep, low or excessive levels of physical activity, abdominal obesity, poor diet) factors that can create inflammation, that make the joint sensitive, swollen, stiff and sore.
  • This contrasting view reinforces the critical role of non-surgical approaches in managing knee OA.
  • Knee pain is a modifiable symptom related to sensitized knee structures and influenced by a variety of biopsychosocial factors rather than solely related to damaged joint structures.
  • Levels of pain and disability are often poorly explained by the degree of structural change on imaging.
  • Symptoms are influenced by a person's context, including life stage, psychological, social, physical and lifestyle factors, and health comorbidities.
  • We shift the focus away from a 'structural damage' model, where a TKR or other surgery is inevitable, towards a 'person-centred' approach, where modifiable biopsychosocial factors that drive pain and disability can be targeted through non-surgical interventions.
  • These guideline-recommended strategies for managing knee OA include providing personally relevant education, prescribing targeted exercise therapy and physical activity aligned with the patient's goals, and providing weight loss support.
  • This change in treatment focus enables clinicians to reassure patients that exercising and strengthening their knees is safe and that they can effectively manage their condition without surgical intervention.
  • There is growing evidence to support this view.
  • Notwithstanding, surgical care may be indicated for patients with advanced OA who have not responded to appropriate non-surgical care and are predicted to respond well to TKR.
  • We help patients build a positive mindset towards a biopsychosocial understanding of knee OA, reinforcing the importance of long-term self-management, including leg-strengthening exercises, functional restoration and regular physical activity to manage the condition effectively instead of over-dependence on passive therapies and quick fixes.
  • Influences on RA development include gut microbiome, oral microbiome (periodontal disease), cold environment, 1st and 2nd-hand smoke exposure, Shift work, Air pollution, Oral contraceptives (lower risk), Breastfeeding (lower risk), Obesity, especially Abdominal obesity, Low vitamin D, Infection (COVID, Epstein Barr Virus), Chemicals (Endocrine-disrupting chemicals or EDCs: Bisphenol A, phthalates, Polychlorinated biphenyls, flame retardants), Androgen deprivation therapy (for prostate cancer), Immune checkpoint inhibitors and Statins.
  • RA and similar inflammatory arthritis are typically treated with medicines such as disease-modifying rheumatic drugs, biologics, and occasionally passive physiotherapy. Evidence supports an integrative, root-cause rehabilitation approach that addresses the physiological imbalances.

RECOUP'S Rheumatological Rehabilitation Team

  • Orthopedic Surgeon
  • Rehabilitation Physicians
  • Regenerative Medicine Physicians
  • Pain Physician
  • Manual Musculoskeletal Physician
  • Occupational Health Physician
  • Orthopedic Rheumatologists
  • Functional and Lifestyle Medicine Physicians
  • Ayurveda Physician
  • Naturopathy Physicians
  • Physiotherapists
  • Occupational Therapists
  • Ergonomists
  • Aquatic Therapists
  • Yoga Therapists
  • Tai Chi and Qi Gong Therapists
  • Alexander and Feldenkrais Teachers
  • Clinical Psychologists
  • Nutritionists
  • Health Coaches
  • Pain Nurses
  • Patient Care Coordinators
  • Other professionals, as required

Features of the RECOUP RR Approach

  • Patient-centered approach.
  • Personalized treatment is based on an accurate clinical and clinometric evaluation of the comorbidities.
  • Conducted at an early stage, differentiated according to the various rheumatic diseases and the disease's different phases and stages.
  • Multimodal and comprehensive rather than sequential monotherapy.
  • Based on a regional as well as a global approach.

Aims of RR

  • Educate the person with arthritis about the condition and its treatment options and fully involve the informed patient in all decision-making relating to treatment.
  • Control pain.
  • Optimize function, reduce activity limitation and participation restriction and improve quality of life.
  • Beneficially modify the arthritis process.
  • Prevent and manage flare-ups.

Importance of early rehabilitation

  • It is more effective for the reduction of pain and joint inflammation.
  • It may prevent joint destruction and associated disability and affect disease progression.
  • At an advanced stage, it may only reduce activity limitations and prevent further joint damage.

Personalization and differentiation of the rehabilitation process

  • Initially, the rehabilitation program includes treatments targeted to the main joint alterations to relieve pain and prevent joint damage and deformity in the most affected areas.
  • Afterwards, it includes a comprehensive rehabilitation plan to improve posture, muscle strength, and the patient's general health status.
  • Also, exercise intensity should be adequately planned based on the maximum heart rate (MHR) when each rehabilitation plan is designed.
  • Exercise intensity may be low (40-54% MHR), moderate (55-69% MHR) or high (>70% MHR) depending on the patient's overall health status, the rheumatic disease and its comorbidities.
  • Also, the type of exercise (static or dynamic), the number of repeats, the duration and frequency of the workout sessions, and the total duration of the rehabilitation process should be personalized according to each patient's needs and problems.

Risk Stratification

  • Around 20% of patients continue to have pain after a TKR.
  • Pre-operative factors were identified as significant predictors of persistent pain after a TKR:
  1. A greater number of pain sites
  2. Higher levels of pre-operative pain
  3. Higher levels of catastrophizing
  4. Higher levels of depression and anxiety
  5. Poorer levels of pre-operative function.

Results of Rehabilitation

  • 75% of people eligible for TKR declined the operation at 12 months. Link: Skou ST, Roos EM, Laursen MB, Rathleff MS, Arendt-Nielsen L, Simonsen O, et al. A Randomized, Controlled Trial of Total Knee Replacement. N Engl J Med. 2015;373(17):1597-606. https://doi.org/10.1056/nejmoa1505467
  • 68% declined it at 24 months. Link: Skou, S.T. et al. Total knee replacement and non-surgical treatment of knee osteoarthritis: 2-year outcome from two parallel randomized controlled trials Osteoarthritis and Cartilage, Volume 26, Issue 9, 1170 – 1180: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.joca.2018.04.014

RECOUP's proprietary Digital Tools

  1. RECOUP Entire Person Health Score and Care Plan Generation using AI: EPHS-S, EPHS-P, EPHS-O
  2. Virtual Ergonomic Workplace Analysis Tool
  3. App-based psychological interventions, e.g., Emotional Awareness and Expression Therapy, Pain Reprocessing Therapy, Acceptance and Commitment Therapy, Progressive Goal Attainment Program
  4. Personalized exercise prescriptions
  5. Targeted pain education based on the phenotype
  6. Lifestyle medicine assessment and interventions
  7. Machine learning-based application to identify Myofascial Trigger Points and provide guidance regarding self-management of Myofascial Pain Syndrome
  8. DEEPAK SHARAN'S PAIN PHENOTYPE SCORE: This acronym represents the first letters of factors affecting pain severity and prognosis. Patients are classified into different phenotypes and endotypes to characterize diseases better and select more precise therapeutic and management approaches.

DEEPAK SHARAN'S Protocol for Chronic Pain Treatment

This protocol is used for RR, with a few additions described below.

Regenerative Medicine

  • Regenerative Medicine (RM) is an emerging and rapidly growing field within medicine that combines advances in tissue engineering and molecular biology to replace or regenerate human cells, tissues, or organs to restore or establish normal function following loss due to injury, disease, or ageing.
  • RM involves introducing stem cells, progenitor cells, biologically active molecules, or the implantation of bioengineered scaffolds or ex vivo-grown tissues.
  • RM aims to restore and establish normal function by enhancing tissue restoration through specially tailored rehabilitation protocols.

Regenerative interventions include

  • Prolotherapy
  • Perineural injections
  • Platelet-rich plasma
  • Autologous whole blood
  • Mesenchymal stem cells
  • Hyaluronic acid
  • Ozone injections
  • Molecular hydrogen therapy

Musculoskeletal Rehabilitation

Musculoskeletal Interventions include

  • Mechanotherapy
  • High-frequency, low-intensity whole-body vibration therapy
  • Pulsed electromagnetic field therapy
  • Aquatic Therapy in Temperature-Controlled Pool
  • Tendon Fenestration Therapy
  • Dry Needling or Trigger Point Injections
  • Pain Intervention Procedures, e.g., Nerve blocks
  • Topical, oral, and injectable pharmacotherapy
  • Psychological and behavioral approaches
  • Lifestyle coaching: nutrition, sleep, neuromuscular exercises, physical activity, weight loss, body mechanics, ergonomics
  • Education in self-help and pain-coping skills
  • Orthotics

Prolotherapy

  • Prolotherapy, also known as regenerative injection therapy, sclerotherapy, proliferation therapy, ligament reconstruction therapy, and fibro-osseous injection therapy, is a treatment of tissue with the injection of an irritant solution into a joint space, weakened ligament, or tendon insertion to relieve pain.
  • It is a recognised orthopaedic procedure that stimulates the body's natural healing processes to strengthen joints decompensated by traumatic or overuse injury.
  • Dr George Hackett popularised prolotherapy in the 1950s.
  • When ligaments or tendon attachments are stretched or torn, joints become hypermobile and painful. Traditional approaches, such as surgery and anti-inflammatory drugs, often fail to stabilise the joint and permanently relieve pain.
  • Prolotherapy, with its unique ability to directly address the cause of the instability, can repair the weakened sites and produce new fibrous tissues, resulting in permanent stabilisation of the joint.
  • Prolotherapy involves the injection of hyperosmolar 15-25% Dextrose (sugar) and local anaesthetic (usually Lignocaine). Other substances may include phenol or sodium morrhuate.
  • Prolotherapy can be used to treat different joints, such as the shoulder, wrist, elbow, spine, sacroiliac joints, temporomandibular joint, hip, knee, and ankle.

Benefits of Prolotherapy:

  • Treatment is minimally invasive and quick (minutes)
  • Cost-effective
  • Provides long-term pain relief
  • Quick recovery and return to activity
  • It does not require general or regional anesthesia
  • Usually results in reduction of long-term medication use
  • Safe
  • Minimal side-effects

Perineural Injections

  • It is also known as Neural Prolotherapy, Neurofascial Prolotherapy or Subcutaneous Prolotherapy.
  • Popularised by New Zealand Physician Dr John Lyftogt.
  • The technique involves injecting 5% dextrose in a sterile water solution around the subcutaneous nerves to promote regeneration, repair, or other functional restoration.
  • Perineural Injections are thought to have a capsaicin/vanilloid receptor 1 (TRPV1) antagonist effect, block neuropathic pain, and reduce neurogenic inflammation in the small subcutaneous nerves that can be the source of chronic pain.

Growth Factors

  • Injection of growth factors and cytokines has become an emerging option in the field of RM.
  • The most common methods are platelet-rich plasma (PRP) and autologous whole-blood injections.
  • Blood contains red blood cells, white blood cells, plasma, and platelets. Red blood cells are responsible for transporting oxygen and carbon dioxide. White blood cells are responsible for inflammatory responses. Plasma carries cells, fluid, nutrients, electrolytes, proteins, clotting factors, and hormones.
  • Platelets are known to be responsible for haemostasis (stopping bleeding) but also release bioactive proteins that promote tissue regeneration and healing and eliminate waste.
  • The cellular component of blood consists of 93% red blood cells, 6% platelets, and 1% white blood cells.

Platelet Rich Plasma

  • PRP is prepared by centrifuging peripheral blood and extracting a concentration of platelets 3-8 times the physiologic norm.
  • PRP is an attractive source of growth factors since it can be easily obtained from a patient's blood at the bedside. It contains elevated concentrations of TGF-b (which increases collagen I production), PDGF (which induces chemotaxis of stem cells), epidermal growth factor (which stimulates connective tissue growth), vascular endothelial growth factors, and fibroblast growth factor-2 (which stimulates angiogenesis).
  • PRP has been used to treat tendinopathy, osteoarthritis, and muscle and ligamentous injury with good results.

Stem Cells

  • Mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) are undifferentiated multipotent stem cells found in the adult bone marrow that can differentiate into bone, cartilage, adipose, tendon, and muscle cells.
  • There is great hope that these cells can treat diseased or injured musculoskeletal tissue. Sources of stem cells include bone marrow, muscle, fat, the umbilical cord, and other tissues. However, it is beneficial to select stem cells that are typically more similar to the differentiated target tissue.
  • Autologous bone marrow concentrate (BMC) is the largest and most accessible source for allogeneic MSCs.
  • Synovial tissue, periosteum, and adipose tissue are other potential sources.

Hyaluronic acid

  • Hyaluronic acid (HA) is a ubiquitous molecule with a high molecular weight naturally in the tendons, joint cartilage, and synovial fluid.
  • It provides joint lubrication, serves as a space filler to keep the joint open, and regulates cellular activities such as protein binding.
  • During the progression of OA, the endogenous HA in the joint is depolymerised from being of high molecular weight (6500–10,900 kDa) into a lower molecular weight (2700–4500 kDa), resulting in reduced mechanical and viscoelastic properties of the synovial fluid in the affected joint.
  • Exogenous HA injections have been used by intra-articular and peri-articular routes to provide pain relief in patients with early and moderate OA and other musculoskeletal conditions.
  • HA's mechanism of action includes the synthesis of proteoglycan and/or glycosaminoglycan, an anti-inflammatory effect, and maintenance of viscoelasticity.

Most regenerative procedures, except Prolotherapy and HA, are relatively recent and lack high-level evidence. In situations where other interventions are contraindicated or ineffective, RM may be a helpful alternative. Since the safety profile of regenerative procedures is favorable, it may allow a patient to avoid the higher risks of undergoing surgical intervention.

Mechanotherapy

  • Defined as "any intervention that introduces mechanical forces to alter molecular pathways and induce a cellular response that enhances tissue growth, modelling, remodelling, or repair."
  • Mechanotherapy delivers mechanical signals (extrinsically, e.g., with physical modalities such as low-intensity pulsed ultrasound, and intrinsically, e.g., via therapeutic exercise prescription, soft tissue and joint mobilisations, and muscle or tendon stretching) to create a tissue force that evokes a cellular and molecular response.
  • This process, in turn, activates specific biological responses in tissues to enhance the integration, healing, and restorative capacity of implanted cells, tissues, or synthetic scaffolds.
  • Mechanotransduction refers to the body's process of converting mechanical loading into cellular responses. These cellular responses, in turn, promote structural change.
  • Mechanotransduction is an ongoing physiological process in the human body, just like respiration and circulation.
  • Without activity, the mechanotransduction signal is weak, so connective tissue is lost (e.g., osteoporosis). When there are loads above the tissue's set point, there is a stimulus through mechanotransduction so that the body adapts by increasing protein synthesis and adding tissue where possible (larger, stronger bone).
  • The tendon is a dynamic, mechanoresponsive tissue that can respond favourably to controlled loading after injury.
  • Muscles are highly responsive to changes in functional demands by modulating load-induced pathways. For muscle injury, controlled loading is done after a brief rest period to allow the scar tissue to stabilize. The benefits of loading include improved alignment of regenerating myotubes, faster and more complete regeneration, and minimization of atrophy of surrounding myotubes.
  • Isolated full-thickness articular cartilage defect of the patella has shown significant pain relief following periosteal transplantation and continuous passive motion.

High-frequency, low-intensity whole-body vibration therapy

  • High-frequency, low-intensity whole-body vibration therapy (WBVT) mechanically stimulates musculoskeletal cells without significant tissue deformation forces.
  • It has been proposed that WBVT induces acceleration of the cell nucleus, which may activate mechanosensitive signalling pathways, as the internal actin cytoskeleton tethers the nucleus.
  • WBVT improves bone strength by increasing bone formation and decreasing bone resorption.
  • WBVT helps treat sarcopenia, which increases skeletal fragility and fall risk in the elderly.

Pulsed Electro-Magnetic Field Therapy

  • Pulsed Electromagnetic Field Therapy (PEMF) or an intelligent magnetic resonance stimulation system is a reparative technique for promoting bone and soft tissue healing.
  • PEMF uses directed pulsed magnetic fields through injured tissue, stimulating cellular repair.
  • PEMF therapy improves the cell's metabolism by stimulating the ion channel openings in the cell membrane.
  • It reduces swelling, decreases irritability, stimulates tissue healing, promotes nerve regeneration, strengthens the bone and promotes fracture healing.
  • FDA has approved PEMF, and it has no significant adverse effects.

Indications for PEMF:

  • To promote bone healing and treat delayed union and non-union of fractures.
  • Acute pain and oedema in superficial soft tissues, e.g., sports injuries.
  • Modulation of muscle tone and spasm.
  • Nerve injuries and compressive neuropathies.
  • Chronic musculoskeletal pain due to Tendinopathies, Fibromyalgia, Arthritis, Disc Degeneration and Radiculopathy, etc.
  • Non-healing wounds.
  • Osteoporosis.

Over 400 publications in indexed, peer-reviewed medical journals validated PEMF's results for these indications.

Aquatic Therapy in Temperature-Controlled Pool

  • Aquatic Therapy (AT) refers to water-based treatments or exercises with therapeutic intent, particularly those for pain modulation, management of connective tissue injury, musculoskeletal rehabilitation, conditioning, and training.
  • AT facilitates muscle relaxation, increases peripheral circulation, and improves body awareness, balance, and trunk stability.
  • It speeds the physical recovery process, allowing early movement, and prevents the development of additional crosslinks in fascial tissue, which can decrease its elastic properties.
  • Mechanical tensile stimulation due to pressure exerted by water can significantly improve stem cell proliferation activity in the fascia and strength of these connective tissues through mechanotransduction, microcirculation, and growth differentiation factor/hormonal response.
  • RECOUP's Aquatic Therapists are trained in Halliwick, Watsu, Ai Chi, Bad Ragaz and several other treatment techniques specific to arthritis, pain management and sports injuries under Sarah Wratten, Aquatic Therapy Tutor and Physiotherapist, UK; and Brinda Merchant, India.

Tendon Fenestration Therapy

  • It refers to the percutaneous use of a needle to treat the tendinopathy.
  • Repetitively passing the needle through the area of tendinopathy disrupts the chronic degenerative process, causes bleeding and inflammation and locally increases growth factors and other substances that promote healing.
  • Prolotherapy, PRP and autologous whole-blood injections can also be used after Tendon Fenestration Therapy.

Intra articular Ozone Injection

  • Intra-articular administration of medical-grade ozone reduces pain, improves joint mobility, has protective immune effects, reduces inflammation and reduces oxidative stress.
  • It reduces the release of proteolytic enzymes and pro-inflammatory cytokines, leading to a decrease in intra-articular edema.
  • It can stimulate joint repair, increase vascularization and promote new cartilage growth.
  • It can delay the degeneration of articular cartilage.
  • It is used in OA, RA and most other types of arthritis.

Major Auto-Hemotherapy (MAHT)

  • MAHT involves injecting ozone gas into blood drawn from a patient.
  • The ozone is allowed to mix with the blood for some time.
  • The ozonated blood is then intravenously infused back into the same patient.
  • It is postulated that MAHT modulates the immune response to autologous antigens involved in histamine release from mast cells and basophils.

Extracorporeal blood oxygenation and ozonation (EBOO)

  • Also called intelligent ozone therapy.
  • EBOO uses a closed-circuit filtration system to remove blood from the patient's body, filter it, ozonate it, pass it through UV and LED light, and then return it to the patient simultaneously.
  • EBOO improves blood flow, reduces chronic inflammation, and facilitates cellular healing and cellular oxygen utilization.

Prolozone Therapy

  • It involves injecting a combination of ozone gas, collagen-producing substances (25% dextrose prolotherapy), and pain portal blocks (hexadiluted lignocaine and clonidine) into the joint.
  • The ozone gas stimulates the body's natural healing process, which can help with pain, inflammation, and tissue regeneration.

Molecular Hydrogen Therapy

  • Hydrogen infusions have been recently used for OA and RA.
  • It could reduce apoptosis response and extracellular matrix degradation in human chondrocytes by inhibiting the activation of the JNK signaling pathway.
  • Hydrogen may play a protective role in RA development by reducing oxidative stress.

Proximal fibular osteotomy (PFO)

  • PFO is a relatively new, minimally invasive surgical procedure that can help relieve knee pain and improve function in patients with mild to moderate severity of medial compartment OA.
  • PFO shifts the loading force from the medial side to the lateral side.
  • PFO can also help improve the varus deformity (bowlegs) and slow the progression of OA.
  • A small piece of the fibula is removed through a small incision, limited dissection, and no internal fixation.
  • Studies have shown that PFO can significantly improve pain, function, and other metrics.
  • The femorotibial angle improves by around 7°.
  • The hip-knee ankle angle improves by around 6°.

Ayurveda

  • Ayurvedic treatments for arthritis can include herbs and supplements, dietary changes, yoga, lifestyle modifications, massage therapies, detoxification (Panchakarma), meditation, and exercise under the supervision of an Ayurvedic physician.
  • Studies on ayurvedic practices have shown that they reduce inflammation, ease RA symptoms and limit flare-ups.

Additional integrative, root cause approaches in RR

  • Diet: focusing on healthy oils, spices, antioxidants
  • Addressing intestinal dysbiosis, probiotics
  • Supplements: anti-inflammatory, chondroprotective, micronutrients
  • Weight loss
  • Addressing lifestyle factors
  • Avoiding environmental toxins
  • Detoxication
  • Mitochondrial regeneration: hot-cold therapy, infrared sauna,
  • Vagal nerve stimulation
  • Yoga, Tai Chi, Qi Gong, Neurocognitive Imagery (Franklin Method)
  • Heart rate variability training, EMG, somatic biofeedback and neurofeedback
  • Gait analysis, foot pressure analysis and customized insoles
  • Orthotics
  • Ambulatory aids

Dr Deepak Sharan is trained and certified in Orthopedic Rheumatology by the Indian Orthopedic Rheumatology Association and the Indian Academy of Orthopedic Surgeons. Other physicians are certified in Rheumatology by the European Alliance of Associations for Rheumatology (EULAR).

Dr Deepak Sharan is trained and certified in Ozone and Hydrogen therapy, Pain Portal Blocks and Proximal Fibular Osteotomy by the Indian Academy of Orthopedic Surgeons.

Dr Deepak Sharan has received Basic and Advanced theoretical and hands-on training in Regenerative Medicine, including Prolotherapy, Perineural Injections, PRP and Stem Cells from the following trainers affiliated with the Hackett Hemwall Patterson Foundation (the world's premier organization for prolotherapy training) and trained under the educational prolotherapy standards developed at the University of Wisconsin (USA):

  • Prof. Suad Trebinjac, Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation, Dubai, UAE.
  • Dr Andrea Piccozzi, Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation, Orthopedic Manual Medicine, Trieste, Italy.
  • Dr. Antonio Angone, Manual Medicine Physician and Osteopath, Bologna, Italy.

Dr Sharan has been practicing RM extensively since 2016 and RR since 2000.
